questions.text: In the client-server model used by YouTube, which of the following is the most accurate description of where a creator's videos are actually stored?

options.text: On the creator's phone or computer, shared directly to viewers
On Google's servers in data centres around the world, controlled by Google
Distributed across all viewers' devices using peer-to-peer technology
On neutral government-owned servers in India that YouTube rents

questions.explanation: Explanation: In the client-server model, when a creator uploads a video to YouTube, the file travels from their device (client) to Google's servers (server). Google stores it across multiple data centres globally - but all of those data centres are owned and controlled by Google. The creator's device merely provided the original content. From that point forward, Google controls the video entirely: its encoding, distribution, access, monetisation, and existence. Option A describes a P2P model (like BitTorrent). Option C is technically what blockchain video platforms like Livepeer attempt. Option D is incorrect - YouTube uses its own proprietary infrastructure, not government servers.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

Explanation: In the client-server model, when a creator uploads a video to YouTube, the file travels from their device (client) to Google's servers (server). Google stores it across multiple data centres globally - but all of those data centres are owned and controlled by Google. The creator's device merely provided the original content. From that point forward, Google controls the video entirely: its encoding, distribution, access, monetisation, and existence. Option A describes a P2P model (like BitTorrent). Option C is technically what blockchain video platforms like Livepeer attempt. Option D is incorrect - YouTube uses its own proprietary infrastructure, not government servers.

questions.text: A YouTube creator has 800,000 subscribers. Google suspends her account. Which of the following BEST describes what happens to her subscriber relationships?

options.text: Subscriber relationships are stored on subscribers' own devices, so they remain intact
Subscribers automatically receive a notification and can re-follow her on another platform
Subscriber relationships are stored in Google's centralised database, so they are severed instantly when the account is suspended
Subscribers keep access to all her old videos through their own YouTube history

questions.explanation: Explanation: In YouTube's centralised system, the subscriber relationship is not a direct connection between creator and subscriber. It is a database record in Google's central user table: {subscriber_id → channel_id, status: active}. When Google suspends the channel, the channel no longer exists in the database (or is marked inaccessible). All 800,000 subscriber records become invalid - not because subscribers did anything, but because the central record they were linked to was modified. This illustrates perfect platform lock-in: the creator built 800,000 relationships, but never owned any of them. They were always Google's database entries. Option D is incorrect because access to videos is also revoked upon account suspension.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation: In YouTube's centralised system, the subscriber relationship is not a direct connection between creator and subscriber. It is a database record in Google's central user table: {subscriber_id → channel_id, status: active}. When Google suspends the channel, the channel no longer exists in the database (or is marked inaccessible). All 800,000 subscriber records become invalid - not because subscribers did anything, but because the central record they were linked to was modified. This illustrates perfect platform lock-in: the creator built 800,000 relationships, but never owned any of them. They were always Google's database entries. Option D is incorrect because access to videos is also revoked upon account suspension.

questions.text: What is a "Single Point of Failure" (SPOF) in a centralised system?

options.text: A programming bug that causes occasional errors in the application
A user error that brings down the entire system temporarily
A component whose failure causes the entire system to stop working, with no fallback alternative
A security vulnerability that allows hackers to access one user's data

questions.explanation: Explanation: A Single Point of Failure (SPOF) is a specific architectural problem - a component that, if it fails, brings down the entire system because the system has no redundant alternative for that component. In centralised systems, the central server or authentication database is the SPOF. When YouTube's auth database has an issue, every user is affected - not just those on a particular server region. This is distinct from a bug (which may only affect certain features) or a security vulnerability (which may only affect one user). The SPOF concept is why Mumbai's October 2020 blackout was so devastating - the Kalwa-Padghe transmission line was a SPOF in the power grid.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation: A Single Point of Failure (SPOF) is a specific architectural problem - a component that, if it fails, brings down the entire system because the system has no redundant alternative for that component. In centralised systems, the central server or authentication database is the SPOF. When YouTube's auth database has an issue, every user is affected - not just those on a particular server region. This is distinct from a bug (which may only affect certain features) or a security vulnerability (which may only affect one user). The SPOF concept is why Mumbai's October 2020 blackout was so devastating - the Kalwa-Padghe transmission line was a SPOF in the power grid.

questions.text: Why does Google store files in Google Drive across multiple data centres, yet this redundancy does NOT protect users from the kind of account suspension Priya experienced?

options.text: Multiple data centres actually make account suspension slower, not faster
Multiple data centres are only for users outside India
Multi-data-centre redundancy protects against hardware failure but does nothing to protect against a central authority's deliberate decision to revoke access
Google's data centres are actually not redundant; each file is stored only once

questions.explanation: Explanation: This is a critical distinction that most people miss. Google stores files redundantly - if one server fails, another has the copy. This protects against hardware failure and provides high availability. However, it does nothing to protect against intentional access revocation. When Google decides to suspend an account, the access revocation is applied to all data centres simultaneously. Having 3 copies of a file means nothing if the access control system says "this user cannot access this file" - and that control system is itself centralised. True protection requires not just redundant storage, but decentralised control - where no single entity has the authority to revoke access. This is what blockchain storage (IPFS + on-chain ownership) provides.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation: This is a critical distinction that most people miss. Google stores files redundantly - if one server fails, another has the copy. This protects against hardware failure and provides high availability. However, it does nothing to protect against intentional access revocation. When Google decides to suspend an account, the access revocation is applied to all data centres simultaneously. Having 3 copies of a file means nothing if the access control system says "this user cannot access this file" - and that control system is itself centralised. True protection requires not just redundant storage, but decentralised control - where no single entity has the authority to revoke access. This is what blockchain storage (IPFS + on-chain ownership) provides.

questions.text: A company is designing a file storage system for 10 million Indian small businesses. The CEO says "we'll just use Google Drive - it's reliable and has redundant servers." As a blockchain developer, which response BEST identifies the architectural risk this decision creates?

options.text: Google Drive is too slow for 10 million businesses and should be replaced with a faster server
Google Drive is not available in India, so it cannot be used

Using Google Drive creates a centralised dependency - Google can suspend accounts, comply with government requests, change pricing, or shut down the product, affecting all 10 million businesses simultaneously with no recourse

questions.explanation: Explanation: This question tests your ability to apply architectural thinking to a real business decision - a key skill for a blockchain developer. Google Drive's technical reliability (uptime, speed, redundancy) is excellent. The architectural risk is not technical performance - it is governance and control. By choosing Google Drive, the company is making all 10 million businesses dependent on Google's continued goodwill, pricing decisions, government compliance (India's DPDP Act could mandate that Google provide business data to Indian authorities), and product strategy (Google has killed many products - Google+, Stadia, Inbox, Allo). A decentralised alternative (IPFS + on-chain ownership) would mean no single entity can affect all businesses simultaneously. This is the correct professional framing for recommending blockchain storage solutions.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation: This question tests your ability to apply architectural thinking to a real business decision - a key skill for a blockchain developer. Google Drive's technical reliability (uptime, speed, redundancy) is excellent. The architectural risk is not technical performance - it is governance and control. By choosing Google Drive, the company is making all 10 million businesses dependent on Google's continued goodwill, pricing decisions, government compliance (India's DPDP Act could mandate that Google provide business data to Indian authorities), and product strategy (Google has killed many products - Google+, Stadia, Inbox, Allo). A decentralised alternative (IPFS + on-chain ownership) would mean no single entity can affect all businesses simultaneously. This is the correct professional framing for recommending blockchain storage solutions.

questions.text: Statement: "Google Drive is safe for storing important business data because Google backs up files across multiple servers, ensuring your data is never lost."
questions.explanation: Google's multi-server redundancy protects against one specific risk: hardware failure. If a server physically breaks, another copy of the file exists. However, "never lost" is false in a critical sense: Google can - and does - delete data when it suspends accounts for policy violations, including false positives from automated systems. Beyond deletion, business data stored on Google Drive is subject to Google's Terms of Service, which allow Google to scan, analyse, and share data with law enforcement under court order or government request. For Indian businesses, this means data stored on Google Drive is subject to both US law (FISA, CLOUD Act) and Indian law. The risk for business data is not primarily hardware failure - it is governance failure: loss of access due to suspension, government request, Google's business decisions (e.g., changing pricing or discontinuing the product), or a security breach of Google's own systems. A genuinely safe solution for important business data requires decentralised storage with cryptographic ownership proof, where no single entity can revoke access.
questions.modelAnswer:
FALSE
✅ Model Justification (Full 2 Marks)

Google's multi-server redundancy protects against one specific risk: hardware failure. If a server physically breaks, another copy of the file exists. However, "never lost" is false in a critical sense: Google can - and does - delete data when it suspends accounts for policy violations, including false positives from automated systems. Beyond deletion, business data stored on Google Drive is subject to Google's Terms of Service, which allow Google to scan, analyse, and share data with law enforcement under court order or government request. For Indian businesses, this means data stored on Google Drive is subject to both US law (FISA, CLOUD Act) and Indian law. The risk for business data is not primarily hardware failure - it is governance failure: loss of access due to suspension, government request, Google's business decisions (e.g., changing pricing or discontinuing the product), or a security breach of Google's own systems. A genuinely safe solution for important business data requires decentralised storage with cryptographic ownership proof, where no single entity can revoke access.

1 Mark: Correctly states FALSE and explains that multi-server redundancy only protects against hardware failure, not account suspension or access revocation
+1 Mark: Identifies additional risks: government data requests, ToS violations, Google's business decisions, and security breaches - with specific reference to Indian business context

questions.text: Statement: "A YouTube creator with 1 million subscribers has a more stable and secure income than a salaried employee at a mid-sized company, because the creator has 1 million direct relationships with fans who support their work."
questions.explanation: The statement is false on a fundamental architectural level. A YouTube creator does NOT have 1 million direct relationships with fans - they have 1 million database entries in Google's centralised user table. These relationships are owned by Google, not the creator. The creator cannot export their subscriber list, cannot contact subscribers directly, cannot take them to another platform, and cannot prevent Google from severing all 1 million relationships simultaneously with a single database update. A salaried employee, by contrast, has a legal contract that requires notice periods, severance pay, and in India, protections under the Industrial Disputes Act. A creator has no such legal protection - the Terms of Service they agreed to explicitly give Google the right to terminate without notice or compensation. Furthermore, creator income depends entirely on: (1) Google's algorithm showing their content, (2) Google's monetisation policies, (3) advertiser budgets, and (4) Google's account moderation - all centralised decisions the creator cannot influence. A salaried employee's income depends on one company's financial health; a YouTuber's income depends on one company's algorithm, policy, and business decisions - none of which the creator has any rights to influence or appeal.
questions.modelAnswer:
FALSE
✅ Model Justification (Full 2 Marks)

The statement is false on a fundamental architectural level. A YouTube creator does NOT have 1 million direct relationships with fans - they have 1 million database entries in Google's centralised user table. These relationships are owned by Google, not the creator. The creator cannot export their subscriber list, cannot contact subscribers directly, cannot take them to another platform, and cannot prevent Google from severing all 1 million relationships simultaneously with a single database update. A salaried employee, by contrast, has a legal contract that requires notice periods, severance pay, and in India, protections under the Industrial Disputes Act. A creator has no such legal protection - the Terms of Service they agreed to explicitly give Google the right to terminate without notice or compensation. Furthermore, creator income depends entirely on: (1) Google's algorithm showing their content, (2) Google's monetisation policies, (3) advertiser budgets, and (4) Google's account moderation - all centralised decisions the creator cannot influence. A salaried employee's income depends on one company's financial health; a YouTuber's income depends on one company's algorithm, policy, and business decisions - none of which the creator has any rights to influence or appeal.

1 Mark: Correctly states FALSE and identifies that subscriber relationships are Google's database entries, not the creator's - with the key architectural point that Google can sever all 1M relationships instantly
+1 Mark: Compares legal protections (employment contract vs ToS), identifies multiple centralised dependencies (algorithm, monetisation policy, account moderation), and articulates why this makes creator income structurally less stable

questions.text: Statement: "In a blockchain network like MST with 71,000 validators, if one validator goes offline, all users lose access to the network."
questions.explanation: One-sentence explanation (required): In MST Blockchain's distributed architecture, 71,000 validators collectively maintain the network - if one goes offline, the remaining 70,999 continue to validate transactions, produce blocks, and serve users without any interruption, because there is no single point of failure; the network requires only a majority of validators (over 35,500) to function, making it architecturally immune to single-node failures that would cripple centralised systems like YouTube.
questions.modelAnswer:
FALSE
✅ Answer: FALSE (1 Mark)

One-sentence explanation (required): In MST Blockchain's distributed architecture, 71,000 validators collectively maintain the network - if one goes offline, the remaining 70,999 continue to validate transactions, produce blocks, and serve users without any interruption, because there is no single point of failure; the network requires only a majority of validators (over 35,500) to function, making it architecturally immune to single-node failures that would cripple centralised systems like YouTube.

questions.text: Ravi is a Pune-based digital artist with 200,000 YouTube subscribers and ₹60,000/month income. He also stores all his digital artwork (500 files, 80GB total) on Google Drive. He has just received a YouTube account suspension email. Identify and explain every consequence Ravi faces, why each happens architecturally, and what a decentralised alternative would have changed for each consequence.
questions.explanation:
Consequence 1 - Immediate Income Loss (1 mark):
Ravi's ₹60,000/month stops instantly. Architecturally, this happens because YouTube's monetisation system checks account status before serving ads on any video. One status field change in Google's database - account_status = 'suspended' - triggers an automatic halt of all ad serving. No ads served = no revenue. Decentralised alternative: on a dApp built on MST Blockchain, Ravi's monetisation would be handled by a smart contract. Revenue from viewers' payments flows directly to Ravi's wallet address via the contract. No intermediary can halt this flow - the contract executes automatically as coded.Consequence 2 - Subscriber Relationships Severed (1 mark):
All 200,000 subscribers can no longer see Ravi's channel. The subscriber relationship exists as a record in Google's database. When the account is suspended, the channel becomes inaccessible, rendering all subscription records functionally meaningless. Ravi cannot contact subscribers, export their details, or move them to another platform. Decentralised alternative: on a blockchain social graph (similar to Farcaster or MST's on-chain social layer), subscribers follow Ravi's on-chain identity (DID). This relationship is recorded on the blockchain - not in anyone's database. Even if the app interface shuts down, the follower relationship persists on-chain and is accessible from any other compatible application.Consequence 3 - Video Content Inaccessible (1 mark):
All videos Ravi uploaded become inaccessible - even though they are stored on Google's servers in multiple copies. The files exist, but the access control system says "this account may not access or serve these files." Google's CDN will not serve the video stream to any viewer. Decentralised alternative: Videos stored on IPFS are addressed by their content hash (CID), not by an account. The CID for Ravi's video remains valid regardless of any platform's decision. Any IPFS node hosting the content will serve it. No account suspension can make a content-addressed file inaccessible - as long as any node in the network holds it.Consequence 4 - Google Drive Files Inaccessible (1 mark):
Because Google Drive is linked to the same Google account, Ravi's 500 artwork files (80GB) are also inaccessible. His design tools (potentially Google Docs, Google Keep notes) are also gone. This is the cascading failure of centralised single sign-on (SSO) - one account covers everything. Decentralised alternative: Files stored on IPFS/Arweave with cryptographic ownership (signed by Ravi's private key) are not linked to any platform account. Ravi's MST wallet is his identity; it cannot be suspended, and it controls access to all his files independently of any application.Consequence 5 - No Recourse or Appeal (1 mark):
Ravi has no legal contract guaranteeing his income, no human reviewer immediately accessible, and no regulatory body with jurisdiction over Google's content moderation decisions. Google's Terms of Service - which Ravi agreed to without reading - explicitly grant Google the right to terminate without notice or compensation. The "appeals process" is automated for the first several rounds. Decentralised alternative: Smart contracts on MST Blockchain execute according to code that is publicly auditable by Ravi before he joins the platform. Rules cannot change without a community DAO vote. No automated system can unilaterally terminate his participation - any change requires governance consensus from token holders.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Model Answer (Full 5 Marks)

Consequence 1 - Immediate Income Loss (1 mark):
Ravi's ₹60,000/month stops instantly. Architecturally, this happens because YouTube's monetisation system checks account status before serving ads on any video. One status field change in Google's database - account_status = 'suspended' - triggers an automatic halt of all ad serving. No ads served = no revenue. Revenue from viewers' payments flows directly to Ravi's wallet address via the contract. No intermediary can halt this flow - the contract executes automatically as coded.

Consequence 2 - Subscriber Relationships Severed (1 mark):
All 200,000 subscribers can no longer see Ravi's channel. The subscriber relationship exists as a record in Google's database. When the account is suspended, the channel becomes inaccessible, rendering all subscription records functionally meaningless. Ravi cannot contact subscribers, export their details, or move them to another platform. Decentralised alternative: on a blockchain social graph (similar to Farcaster or MST's on-chain social layer), subscribers follow Ravi's on-chain identity (DID). This relationship is recorded on the blockchain - not in anyone's database. Even if the app interface shuts down, the follower relationship persists on-chain and is accessible from any other compatible application.

Consequence 3 - Video Content Inaccessible (1 mark):
All videos Ravi uploaded become inaccessible - even though they are stored on Google's servers in multiple copies. The files exist, but the access control system says "this account may not access or serve these files." Google's CDN will not serve the video stream to any viewer. Decentralised alternative: Videos stored on IPFS are addressed by their content hash (CID), not by an account. The CID for Ravi's video remains valid regardless of any platform's decision. Any IPFS node hosting the content will serve it. No account suspension can make a content-addressed file inaccessible - as long as any node in the network holds it.

Consequence 4 - Google Drive Files Inaccessible (1 mark):
Because Google Drive is linked to the same Google account, Ravi's 500 artwork files (80GB) are also inaccessible. His design tools (potentially Google Docs, Google Keep notes) are also gone. This is the cascading failure of centralised single sign-on (SSO) - one account covers everything. Decentralised alternative: Files stored on IPFS/Arweave with cryptographic ownership (signed by Ravi's private key) are not linked to any platform account. Ravi's MST wallet is his identity; it cannot be suspended, and it controls access to all his files independently of any application.

Consequence 5 - No Recourse or Appeal (1 mark):
Ravi has no legal contract guaranteeing his income, no human reviewer immediately accessible, and no regulatory body with jurisdiction over Google's content moderation decisions. Google's Terms of Service - which Ravi agreed to without reading - explicitly grant Google the right to terminate without notice or compensation. The "appeals process" is automated for the first several rounds. Decentralised alternative: Smart contracts on MST Blockchain execute according to code that is publicly auditable by Ravi before he joins the platform. Rules cannot change without a community DAO vote. No automated system can unilaterally terminate his participation - any change requires governance consensus from token holders.

5/5 marks: All five consequences identified with architectural explanation and decentralised alternative for each
3 - 4 marks: Three to four consequences fully explained with alternatives
1 - 2 marks: Consequences listed without architectural explanation or alternatives
0 marks: Only surface-level description ("he loses his videos") without architectural reasoning
